data.title: Techtronic Industries: Focus On Its Largest Customer
data.description:
I'm staying bullish on Techtronic Industries (TTNDY) (TTNDF) (0669.HK).

Its key client's quarterly results and investor event announcements suggest that TTNDY has a bright future.

My earlier August 7, 2025, article highlighted its above-expectations 1H25 gross profit-to-sales.

Don't Worry About Customer's Growth Deceleration

TTNDY's financial numbers are only published bi-annually. The firm's major partner offers clues about its recent performance.

Its annual report states that "the Group’s largest customer" represented "44.6%" of "total (2024) revenue."

At its FY24 briefing, TTNDY said that "our relationship with Home Depot (HD) is a major reason that we continue to flourish in that (North American) region." An analyst from Citigroup (C) also described "Home Depot" as its "dominant customer" at the call.

It's reasonable for me to infer that HD is TTNDY's top client.

HD's YoY turnover expansion slowed from 2QFY25's (YE end-Jan) +5% to 3QFY25's +3%. The "same store sales growth/SSSG" of +0.2% in the latest three-month period also fell short of the +1.3% consensus projection as per S&P Capital IQ.

But I'm still optimistic about TTNDY's near-term prospects for a number of reasons.

HD indicated at its 3QFY2025 results meeting that "underlying demand in the business remained relatively stable sequentially." It credited the underperformance to "a lack of storms relative to historic norms." This is a one-off, which has little impact on business fundamentals.

Also, Techtronic has outdone its key customer previously.

TTNDY's 1H2025 topline was 7.1% higher on YoY terms. HD registered inferior SSSG of -0.3% and +1.0% for 1QFY25 and 2QFY25, respectively. In my opinion, the disparity is attributable to a difference in sales mix.

HD revealed at a September 2025 Goldman Sachs (GS) forum that "our Home Depot business is equally split about 50-50, the Pro (Professionals) and the consumer."

TTNDY's "Power Equipment" division, which its professionals-focused brand MILWAUKEE falls under, contributed 95% of 2025 interim revenues.

HD's 3QFY25 call remarks suggested that "our pros are reporting months that they're booked out." On the other hand, it drew attention to "ongoing consumer uncertainty."

I think that Techtronic's latest performance is likely to be superior as compared to HD, given the former's relatively greater focus on the pro segment.

Separately, HD's 3rd quarter management commentary noted "big ticket comp (comparable) transactions for those over $1,000 were positive 2.3% compared to the third quarter of last year." It explained that "we continue to see customers trade up for innovation."

The above-mentioned plays into the company's strengths. At the H1 call, TTNDY stressed that "we increased our R&D spend to 4.6% of sales, 50 basis points higher" YoY to support "disruptive innovation, new product development."

TTNDY's latest disclosure reinforces my bullishness regarding its outlook.

It issued a "voluntary announcement" on Dec. 11 without providing any quantitative information. The group shared that its "point of sales (POS) demand trends remained strong across all core verticals through the month of November for both the (professionals-focused) MILWAUKEE and (consumer-focused) RYOBI brands."

HD's Investor Event Has A Favorable Read-Across For TTNDY

HD hosted its "Investor and Analyst Conference" or "Investor Day" on Dec. 10.

The most critical takeaway for me was the upward revision in its "Total Addressable Market/TAM" projection from $1.0 trillion to $1.1 trillion.

HD left its Consumer sub-segment TAM unchanged at $0.5 trillion. But it lifted the Pro estimate by +20% to $600 billion.

That's good news for TTNDY whose MILWAUKEE brand targets this category of clients.

HD's Professional Customer Mapping

As per the chart presented above, HD currently has a limited presence in the "medium/large pro" and "complex project purchase occasion" areas.

At the Investor Day, "medium/large professionals" are profiled as those who "work on complex projects" and belong to "1 of 3 groups: a medium to large renovator or remodeler, single-family custom homebuilder and single-family tract builder."

According to the investor presentation, "50% of the ($0.6 trillion) addressable market opportunity represents pros working on complex projects."

TTNDY referred to "MILWAUKEE" as the "number one professional brand" in its annual report. HD will most probably have to leverage on MILWAUKEE's popularity to penetrate the "medium/large professionals" segment.

At its '25 interim earnings call, Techtronic mentioned its "M18 AutoStop drill driver." This has been designed with an automatic shutoff function, which is activated when the user is at risk of an injury. It emphasized that the tool provides "a major advantage to any contractor that cares about safety on a job site."

You can imagine that TTNDY's "M18 AutoStop" is the kind of sophisticated product that HD needs to grab a bigger slice of the "complex projects" pie.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

Weather conditions can be a substantial factor influencing the short-term results of both Home Depot and Techtronic. This is evidenced by HD's Q3 SSSG miss.

TTNDY could fail to align its future product development with the ambitions of its largest customer. If it prioritizes consumer-focused offerings over professional-related ones in the future, that might prompt HD to seek out alternative suppliers.

The Final Word

My "Buy" rating for the stock remains intact.

I valued "TTNDY at a fair P/E-to-ROE ratio of 1x" in the early-Aug update. I'm sticking to the same approach here. The consensus FY2026 "return on equity" forecast for Techtronic is 19.3%, implying a target P/E of 19.3x.

TTNDY's forward one-fiscal-year earnings multiple is 15.2 times now, as per S&P Capital IQ. The shares boast a potential upside of +27% (19.3/15.2).

I view its prospects for the near term and long run as promising, after analyzing HD's disclosures.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arqit Hasn't Earned The Benefit Of The Doubt, Yet
data.description:
Arqit Quantum (ARQQ) has been a stock I’ve consistently been bearish on since early January. Well, my take on Arqit hasn’t changed, even after a double-digit pullback year to date, down over 23%. I think the hype around quantum stocks has subsided, and valuations are returning to levels where fundamentals actually justify them. In Arqit’s case, that means further downside risk in my view, both on the fundamentals and the technical setup. The stock just broke its strong support at the EMA200 at $30 and EMA21, and is trading at around $28. I see the risk-reward as unfavorable at current levels and would advise investors to watch for confirmation of a rebound.

Arqit is still trading more on hype than on tangible evidence of near-term monetization. I’m maintaining the stock with a sell, and I expect its valuation to continue to normalize lower as investor enthusiasm for the broader quantum theme fades. To keep a long story short, I don’t see a catalyst that re-rates this name upward anytime soon.

Stock performance and past coverage:

Arqit has been getting hammered, along with the broader tech sector, amid revived fears of an imminent AI bubble pop. The stock is down around 10% over the past month, holding on in line with peers, with D-Wave (QBTS) down 8.7%, Rigetti (RGTI) down 21%, Quantum Computing (QUBT) down 1.1% and IonQ (IONQ) down another 6.6%.

Still, Arqit is actually underperforming most peers year to date, down 25.4%, against QBTS up 219%, RGTI up 71.7%, QUBT down 24%, and IONQ up 23.7%.

And while that might sound like a relative buying opportunity on paper, I don’t see it that way. In my view, Arqit’s underperformance isn’t a case of the market overlooking a fundamentally strong name but a market correctly discounting a story that hasn’t delivered. Arqit simply hasn’t earned the benefit of the doubt, and until the company produces clear evidence of sustainable demand, meaningful contract wins, or a credible path to profitability, I believe the stock will continue to lag the quantum peers.

I last covered the stock in late September, when it traded at $41, maintaining my sell from late June, when it traded at $37.7. It is now down 29% and 21% from both these calls, respectively.

Arqit, from where I’m standing, remains a story stock, and earnings a few days ago on the 9th of December did little to change that.

Earnings rundown:

According to the company’s CEO, Andrew Leaver, 2025 “was a year of building momentum for the company as well, momentum in prospective customer engagements across our key markets, momentum in revenue and momentum in contracted backlog coming into fiscal year 2026. Fiscal year 2025 was also a year of broadening our product and service footprint.”

For FY2025, Arqit reported revenue of $530K, missing consensus by $10K. Nearly all of it came in the second half, with H2 revenue rising to $463K versus just $67K in H1. The company generated revenue from seven contracts, including two multi-year agreements that contribute recurring revenue, spanning telecom operators as well as government, defense, and enterprise customers.

Visibility for FY26 is more optimistic, and the company expects to “to generate revenues of approximately $1.2 million in fiscal year 2026,” according to the press release, but “Recognition of revenue under existing contracts is subject to a number of factors including, amongst others, the successful delivery of our products under the terms of those contracts, and the assumption that customers will not cancel, delay or amend the terms their contracts.”

So, again, it all depends on management’s execution and delivery. And that has been the core issue with Arqit from the start. The company talks about momentum, but the numbers continue to tell a much smaller story. The way I see it, even the FY26 guidance of $1.2 million in expected revenue is hardly enough to move the needle for a company still burning $2.5 million per month. The word momentum was mentioned 9 times during the call, and it remains to be seen whether it will materialize into meaningful, repeatable revenue.

Valuation:

Arqit is expensive at current levels. Arqit’s 69x forward sales multiple is not “cheap” by comparison to peers and remains expensive for a company producing just $0.53 million in annual revenue, burning $2.5 million per month, and guiding to only ~$1.2M next year.

Forward growth expectations also favor peers: IonQ is projected to grow 105%, QUBT 80%, and QBTS 65%, while Arqit provides no forward growth estimate. Profitability metrics paint an equally challenging picture. Arqit posts extreme negative returns, ROE at -181% and ROTC at -99.8%, and burns cash at a rate similar to peers despite generating far less revenue.

Conclusion and the risks:

I see more downside ahead and would advise investors to revisit Arqit only if the stock resets to a level that properly reflects its execution risk and lack of visibility. That said, I want to be clear that there is a challenge to my bearish view. On the upside, Arqit could benefit from renewed speculative flows into quantum stocks if sentiment turns bullish, especially if larger peers post headline-grabbing contract wins or governments re-emphasize quantum security spending. A material multi-year contract with a Tier-1 player or a government agency would also make me reconsider my rating. However, at this stage, those remain hypothetical rather than base-case assumptions. More specific to Arqit, though, what would change my view is not only incremental revenue growth or management commentary around “pipeline momentum,” but tangible proof of scale through accelerating contract wins, improving revenue visibility, and a clear reduction in cash burn relative to growth.

Until then, Arqit remains a speculative story stock in my books.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: National Beverage: Strong Margins, But A Slowing Growth Story - Hold
data.description:
Thesis

An unconventional but high-quality Company in the non alcoholic beverage sector is National Beverage Corp. (FIZZ).

The Company is not so much interested in aggressive volume, but profitability.

As i see it, the fiscal Q2 2026 financial results reaffirm the company’s track record and art of maintaining impressive margins despite facing cost pressures and stiff competition.

Fizz isn’t chasing market share via price discounting or huge promotions, but is instead investing in the power of its brands, anchored by LaCroix and other premium sparkling options.

The recent results showed stable sales with a slight increase vs. last year, but with operating efficiencies showing significant improvement.

Management remains focused on value creation through margins and cash flow and it doesn’t mind seeing growth slowing down a bit.

With a P/E of 18x, the stock already prices in much of this quality, limiting the immediate upside margin.

Financials

In Q2 2026, National Beverage recorded a mild increase in net sales on an annual basis, continuing the stabilization picture that had begun to take shape since the Q1 2026.

Revenue growth was at a low single-digit rate, significantly lower than the double-digit levels of previous years but clearly we see a better picture compared to the stagnation observed in a part of fiscal year 2025.

The gross profit margin was further strengthened, thanks to a better sales mix, reduced promotional activities and a gradual de-escalation of certain raw materials and transportation.

Compared with the same quarter last year, gross margins moved higher by several tens of basis points, confirming that the pricing strategy remains effective.

Operating expenses increased modestly, with management showing emphasis in salary costs and advertising expenses.

This resulted in operating profit growing at a faster pace than sales, improving the operating margin compared to both Q2 FY25 and Q1 FY26.

Net profit followed the same trend, with y-o-y growth and an improvement in the net profit margin.

The company showed strong FCF generation, with no significant capital needs and no net debt.

Corporate Developments, Strategy and Management Outlook

National Beverage is committed to expanding its premium portfolio giving emphasis on sparkling water and zero sugar products.

While LaCroix remains the milestone brand, management is accelerating growth in complementary categories such as flavored sparkling waters and functional beverages targeting more specialized consumer audiences.

In recent results, management highlighted that consumer behavior remains cautious, especially in lower income groups.

FIZZ products, however, are also more durable as their consumer base is quality and lifestyle-centric.

The company, through this distinction, avoids aggressive price reductions and protect its profit margins.

Regarding innovation, the company keeps introducing new flavors and limited editions not with the aim of explosive volume growth but to maintain its brand engagement.

This strategy seems effective, supporting the loyalty of clients without significant cost increase.

At the same time the company's management is cautious to its estimates avoiding ambitious guidance for accelerated growth.

Instead, the message focuses on consistency, efficiency and long-term value creation.

The absence of acquisitions and aggressive investment may disappoint investors that are attracted by quick expansion but is aligned with the company's DNA.

Dividend Policy and Capital Returns

National Beverage is not a classic dividend pick, despite its long history of distributions.

The company does not follow a consistent or predictable regular dividend policy. It chooses instead to distribute special dividends when management believes that liquidity and conditions permit it.

The most recent example was the special dividend of $3.25 per share in 2024, while in the past there have been extraordinary payments including $6.00 in 2020 and $3.00 in 2021.

The lack of annual repeatability means that the investor cannot rely on a specific dividend yield as a fixed income element.

Instead, dividends work more as a mechanism for returning excess liquidity when the company does not see decent reinvestment opportunities.

While the balance sheet is strong and free of debt, FIZZ does is not for investors looking for reliable cash income. Dividends are better viewed as an added benefit rather than a foundational investment feature.

Valuation

With a current stock price of $35 and a P/E of 18x, National Beverage is valued at a market cap. of around $3.3 billion, taking into account the 93.62 million shares outstanding.

While this level is slightly below the historical norm, it does not suggest a compelling valuation for investors focused on growth.

In the base case scenario where P/E remain stable and the company continues to expand with low single digit pace, a P/E of 18x is considered fair.

In this case, the stock is trading around current levels, with limited upside but also controlled downside risk, given the strong balance sheet and the absence of debt.

In the bear case scenario competitive pressure intensifies margins are squeezed and growth slows even more, the market could value FIZZ at a P/E of 15x-16x.

With the same earnings, this would move the stock lower (to levels way below $30) reflecting a more conservative stance on the company's prospects. In the bull case scenario, a return to more substantial organic growth combined with margin improvement or new successful products, could justify a P/E of 20x-21x.

In this case, the stock could move towards $40, but without drastically changing its investment profile. Overall, the current valuation supports a hold recommendation, not an aggressive placement.

On NASDAQ, the stock shows this soft investment approach.

As we can see in the chart, the range of fluctuation is narrow.

The downward movement should be noted, although not as aggressive, with FIZZ trading near 6-year lows, implying investor indifference even at such low levels.

The long-term trend is located to $28 (-20%) with the upper level range extending toward $65.

Risks

All investments have their risks. National Beverage's main risk is limited volume growth.

In an industry where larger players invest aggressively in marketing and distribution, FIZZ’s more smooth approach could lead to gradual market share erosion over time.

The second risk relates to the company’s significant dependence on LaCroix. Although the brand continues to perform well, changes in consumer trends or increased competition in premium offerings could weigh more heavily on financial performance.

Diversification efforts are progressing but not fast enough to meaningfully mitigate this exposure.

Cost fluctuations in raw materials, packaging and transportation are source of uncertainty.

If costs rise again, margins could come under pressure, particularly if the company absorbs part of the increase.

Finally, the stock's valuation limits the margin for error. At levels above the historical average for the industry, any disappointing results or sign of a slowdown could lead to a sharp price adjustment.

Conclusion

National Beverage remains a high quality, profitable and financially strong company, supported by a clear strategy and consistent execution.

Recent results confirm the stability of its business model but do not point to an acceleration in growth.

At current valuation levels, the stock offers limited upside but provides a degree of downside protection.

Overall, the picture supports a Hold recommendation for FIZZ stock, with an emphasis on quality rather than rapid returns.

data.title: Medical Properties Trust: The Worst Is Over
data.description:
Although I don't own anywhere near as many shares of Medical Properties Trust (MPW) as I have in the past, I am still bullish about the company, and I still find it to be an interesting prospect. Lately, that bullishness has paid off. Since I last reaffirmed the company as a "Strong Buy" in early September of this year, shares have skyrocketed 29.5%. That's well above the 6.4% increase that the S&P 500 has seen over the same period. That surge might lead some investors to believe that upside is more limited from here. And while it is true that some of the easy money has been captured, I would argue that the company is still cheap enough on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar enterprises to justify the "Strong Buy" rating that I have consistently had on it for a while now.

The worst is over

For the last couple of years now, Medical Properties Trust has really been through the ringer. Issues with tenants, as well as high amounts of debt, have proven problematic for the business. And as I have detailed in other articles, such as my most recent one about it, these issues have created a permanent destruction of shareholder value. Having said that, it looks to me as though the picture is improving. Admittedly, there are some aspects of the company that still have some improvement to make before we are in a really good spot. But with tenant issues largely resolved, and management now focused on returning to growth, I believe that this prospect is certainly worthy of attention.

The income statement is the best place to start when evaluating Medical Properties Trust. According to management, for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, total revenue came in at $237.5 million. This represents an increase over the $225.8 million the company reported a year earlier. Overwhelmingly, this increase was attributable to a 6.6% jump associated with the rent billed to clients. Management mentioned that this rent, as well as straight-line rent that represents a separate line item on the income statement, was driven by the re-tenanting of facilities that were previously owned by its largest tenant, Steward Health Care System. For a couple of years, that tenant had struggled. And since it was the largest tenant that Medical Properties Trust had, the prospect of its bankruptcy, which ultimately occurred in May of 2024, significantly impaired shareholder confidence in the medical property REIT.

Management has been successful in re-tenanting several of these properties. And that has brought in additional rental income. However, it is important to note that the company has made some other changes with some of these assets. At the end of September, for instance, management mentioned that they sold two of their facilities in Phoenix, Arizona, for around $50 million. These were two of the 18 hospitals that had been re-tenanted that had previously been controlled by Steward. Speaking of changes to its properties, the company has recently made some other adjustments. Back in 2022, Yale New Haven Health System agreed to acquire three hospitals from Medical Properties Trust that had been under contract with another major tenant by the name of Prospect Medical Holdings, which also ended up declaring bankruptcy in January of this year.

The purchase price for those hospitals was a whopping $435 million. But ultimately, this arrangement ended up in court, with the buyer fighting to back out because of the condition and management of those three hospitals, with arguments involving unpaid taxes, overdue vendor bills, and the like. In September of this year, Medical Properties Trust and Prospect agreed to a settlement whereby Yale New Haven Health System will pay $45 million to Prospect, with those proceeds ultimately being used to reduce Medical Properties Trust’s debtor-in possession loan that the company issued to Prospect. Those three hospitals have now been freed up to be sold or re-tenanted to other parties. And once a sale, which is what management still plans, is achieved, Medical Properties Trust anticipates that the full $105 million debtor-in-possession loan that it has issued to Prospect will be paid off, with only its $30 million secured conditional loan still outstanding with that troubled tenant.

It seems to me as though these issues are quickly wrapping up all the problems that Medical Properties Trust currently has. And already, the rise in revenue that it's getting from re-tenanted assets is bearing fruit on the bottom line. During the third quarter of 2025, the company reported a net loss of $77.7 million. That's a massive improvement over the $801.2 million net loss reported a year earlier. Operating cash flow did fall from $59.2 million to $18.6 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $33.5 million to $39.5 million. FFO, or funds from operations, did improve from negative $494.5 million to positive $92 million. But on an adjusted basis, we did get a drop from $90.9 million to $77.2 million. Fortunately, however, EBITDA is still on the rise, climbing from $219.3 million to $226.6 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Revenue for this window is still down. And honestly, most cash flow metrics are also. Net profits are up, but that is the least reliable of the profitability metrics when it comes to a REIT. What this demonstrates to me is that the third quarter really marked a turnaround in the business. And what's really exciting is that management expects the combination of rent escalations and re-tenanted properties to push annualized cash rent for the business up to $1 billion by 2026. Even though economic conditions are challenging right now, it's also worth noting that, in the third quarter earnings call, the company said that the EBITDARM of its general acute care operators jumped more than $200 million year over year. This signals stability with its tenants and definitely reduces the risk profile of the business. Post-acute operators also saw an increase year over year, amounting to roughly $50 million. So that is certainly great to see.

Another positive thing about the company is that shares look rather cheap at the moment. In the chart above, you can see its EV-to-EBITDA multiple compared to five other similar firms. With a reading of 14, the company is trading lower than any of these peers. In the charts below, you can see a different way to assess the company. And this is through its price-to-book multiple. On this basis, Medical Properties Trust is drastically lower than any of these comparable firms. If, for instance, it were to trade at the lowest price-to-book multiple of these competitors, that would imply upside of 131.1%. By comparison, implied upside using the EV to EBITDA multiple is considerably lower at 11.4%.

This does not mean that there aren't risks that investors need to be cognizant of. For starters, the net leverage ratio of the business is still quite high. Using annualized EBITDA as of the third quarter of this year, which amounts to $907.4 million, the net leverage ratio for the company has risen to 10.16. This is higher than at any point leading up to this, with the exception of the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. And as the subsequent chart below that illustrates, its net leverage ratio is quite about higher than any of these comparable firms. This most certainly deserves some discount compared to these other businesses. That's why I have long maintained that a more appropriate valuation of the company would be around book value, which would imply upside from current pricing of about 35.1%. But of course, if cash rents due increase, and if that translates to higher cash flows, it's not unthinkable that upside could be more material than that.

One area that investors should continue to watch out for, however, is the overall book value per share of the business and how that changes over time. In the chart below, you can see that shareholders equity amounted to $4.66 billion in the third quarter. That's down from the $4.83 billion reported in the second quarter, and it's meaningfully lower than the $8.59 billion that the company had in the final quarter of 2022. Significant asset write-downs and asset sales have negatively affected the business in this regard. And the book value per share has contracted to $7.75. One thing that I think investors should be the most focused on over the next few fiscal quarters is whether we see some stabilization here. Ideally, we would want to see some improvements. And if, indeed, cash flows do start to rise as annualized rents increase, an increase in this regard is certainly on the table.

My one fear is that, instead of paying down debt, management will start misallocating capital. I am less concerned about acquisitions and more concerned about share buybacks. In the third quarter earnings call, management mentioned that they were initiating a $150 million share buyback program. Given the discount to book value that the stock is trading at, and its all-around discount relative to comparable businesses, I understand the reason behind this maneuver. But at this time, management would be much better off either buying attractive assets without using leverage, or using that capital to reduce overall debt even more. If the firm does start buying large amounts of stock back, that would certainly justify a downgrade in my book. But so far, they have not made any such purchases that I can see. Sometimes, companies do this to create optimism among investors and to signal a longer-term objective. And in those cases, purchases are minimal or nonexistent. Regardless, this is something for investors to watch.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I think that the worst is over for Medical Properties Trust. The company is on better footing now and is achieving better financial performance than in the last several quarters. We have returned to growth, and shares are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to comparable businesses. There are certain risks still, but those are namely centered around the actions that management might take. Unless we see those risks play out, or unless something else changes materially, I believe that maintaining a "Strong Buy" rating is the right choice.

data.title: Credo: AEC Dominance, Pricing Power, And Operating Leverage Justify Premium Valuations
data.description:
I previously covered Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd. (NASDAQ:CRDO) in September 2025, discussing how its triple-digit rally since the April 2025 bottom might be attributed to the expanding hyperscaler partnership and the bottom-line inflection.

Despite the growth tailwinds, it was undeniable that the stock was rather expensive compared to its connectivity peers/historical trends, with the pulled forward upside potential also triggering a minimal margin of safety - resulting in my Hold rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am upgrading the CRDO stock to a Buy, with the recent sideways trading already triggering a notably improved margin of safety, aided by the profitable growth trends arising from the AEC monetization during the multi-year cloud super cycle.

Combined with the hyperscalers' outsized data center capex guidance and the upcoming monetization of the fifth hyperscaler customer, I am of the opinion that the company is likely to remain highly profitable moving forward, aided by the robust pricing power and the improving operating leverage.

CRDO Boasts Robust AI Monetization Cadence

CRDO 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Hold rating, CRDO has hit a new 52 week high around the $213s, before losing much of those gains to confirm the Q4'25 bottom in the $130s ranges over the past two correction cycles.

The bullish support at the $130s is not surprising indeed, since those levels are nearer to my fair value estimates of $133.40 (to be further discussed in the last segment), significantly aided by the growing monetization of its Active Electrical Cable [AEC] offerings across numerous hyperscalers.

It is unsurprising that CRDO's "AECs have become the de facto standard for inter-rack connectivity and are now displacing optical rack-to-rack connections up to 7 meters," attributed to their improved reliability compared to fiber optic cables, allowing customers to avoid "what’s called a link flap, where one part of an AI cluster goes offline because the optical cable connecting them fails, costing hours of pricey GPU time."

These reasons are also why CRDO has been able to monetize four hyperscalers' intensified capex trends, with an additional customer expected to ramp up over the next few quarters - all of which already triggering its dominance in the AEC market at 88% share.

This is thanks to the increased complexity/interconnectivity of the hyperscalers' mega data center build outs where "hundreds of thousands of GPUs" and multi-data center training/inferencing capabilities are seemingly becoming the norm.

This is on top of the denser compute servers and the diversification into intra-rack connections, with it triggering CRDO's outsized AEC monetization opportunities beyond the historical "one cable per server" to "nine cables per server."

Combined with the "step function improvement in reliability and power efficiency that's driving the expansion of the AEC TAM in 100 gig and now 200 gig per lane generations," I am of the opinion that the company is likely to be a beneficiary of the accelerated data center wire/cable market size growth from $20.91B in 2025 to $54.82B in 2031, expanding at a CAGR of +17.4%.

The same has been observed in CRDO's growing FQ2'26 revenues of $268M (+20.1% QoQ/+272.2% YoY) and the robust pricing power at adj gross margins of 67.7% (+4.1 points YoY/+7.1 from FY2022 levels of 60.6%).

This is on top of the management's FQ3'26 revenue guidance of $340M at the midpoint (+26.8% QoQ/+151.8% YoY) and FY2026 revenue growth by +170% YoY, aided by the improved customer mix with "our fourth customer surpassing the 10% revenue threshold for this fiscal year."

The increasingly large revenue base is also why CRDO has been able to report a notably improved operating leverage at adj operating margins of 46.3% (+34.8 points QoQ/+55 from FY2022 levels of -8.7%), with it directly translating to the richer adj EPS performance at $0.67 (+857.1% YoY).

Combined with the richer cash flows and the healthy balance sheet with zero debts, I can understand why the stock has remained well supported at current levels.

CRDO's Reasonably Valued Profitable Growth Trends

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These factors directly tip the scales in favor of CRDO's investment thesis, with the profitable growth trends triggering the consensus accelerated top/bottom-line growth estimates, while building upon the historical 3Y top-line growth at a CAGR of +60.1%.

If anything, readers must note that the current FY2028 revenue estimates of $2B appears rather conservative against JPMorgan Chase's AEC market size estimates of $4B in 2028, with it implying a potential for further beat and raise performances ahead.

These numbers do not appear overly aggressive as well, based on numerous hyperscalers' outsized FY2026 data center capex guidance as Nvidia (NVDA) also offers an aggressive AI market size projection of up to $4T by 2030 - with it highlighting the durable multi-year cloud super cycle spending trends.

CRDO Valuations

Based on the recent stock price movement, it is apparent that CRDO is likely to grow into its premium valuations instead of experiencing a deep pullback, as observed in the moderation in its FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 60.64x from last Hold rating levels of 69.95x, the 1Y mean of 64.44x, and the 5Y mean of 162.36x.

These reasons are also why CRDO appears relatively cheap at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.73x, based on the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 60.64x and the consensus raised 3Y adj EPS growth estimates at +82.3%.

When comparing to its direct connectivity competitors, including:

  • Broadcom (AVGO) at 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.59x (using the same methodology),
  • Arista (ANET) at 2.08x,
  • Synopsys, Inc. (SNPS) at 3.98x,
  • Cadence Design Systems, Inc. (CDNS) at 2.99x, and
  • aside from Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) at 0.70x,

it is apparent that CRDO remains attractively valued against some of its market leading networking peers during the multi-year AI boom, thanks to its profitable growth cadence and the improved margin of safety arising from the recent sideways trading.

CRDO Is An Even Better Buy After Recent Consolidation

CRDO 3Y Stock Price

For now, CRDO has been trading sideways since September 2025 albeit confirming the Q4'25 floor at the $130s as the stock seemingly charts new highs in the $213s along the 50/100 day moving averages.

CRDO Technical Indicators

Thanks to the ongoing consolidation, CRDO's technical indicators appear more reasonable across the RSIs and the trading volumes, with it moderating the stock's prior overbought position in early Q4'25 while offering interested investors with an improved margin of safety.

Based on the annualized FQ2'26 adj EPS performance of $2.68 (+28.8% QoQ/+857.1% YoY) and my last Buy rating P/E levels of 49.80x (nearer to its peer groups' current mean of 46.80x), it is apparent that there remains a notable distance to my fair value estimates of $133.40.

Otherwise, based on the consensus raised FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $4.24, it goes without saying that the recent sideways trading has brought forth an excellent upside potential of +33.6% to my long-term price target of $211.10.

Given the minimal catalysts nearing the end of the Q3'25 earnings season, I am of the opinion that CRDO is likely to continue trading sideways in the near-term as it also grows into the pulled forward upside potential as it has since September 2025.

As a result of the reasonable technical indicators, the established trading pattern, and the robust profitable growth prospects, I am reiterating my previous Buy rating for CRDO, with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer before adding.

With the April 2025 uptrend support line already breached by November 2025, the stock may very well further retrace to retest the 200 day moving averages of $140s. Given the potential -11% correction from current levels, some patience may be more prudent in the meantime for a lower dollar cost average.

data.title: PayPal: Too Late To Sell (Downgrade)
data.description:
My September decision to upgrade PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) back to Strong Buy turned out to be too optimistic as the company's market cap decreased by almost 10% since then while the S&P 500 index (SP500) gained 3% over the same period. The stock received downgrades from BofA Securities and J.P. Morgan over last ten days, and after delving into PYPL's recent performance across business metrics and considering mounting uncertainties in the macroenvironment, I tend to agree with these downgrades.

I don't want to sell PYPL, because I feel that it is too late as it is already extremely cheap and potential positives look more like speculation than investment. With all that being said, buying more shares does not look attractive to me. Therefore, I am downgrading PYPL back to Hold.

Reasons supporting PYPL downgrade

Yesterday, PYPL was downgraded by BofA Securities from Buy to Neutral with a massive target price reduction from $93 to $68. The prominent Wall Street banks are concerned about branded checkout growth despite the management's endeavors to innovate and improve checkout experience for customers. A bit earlier this month, there was a downgrade to Neutral rating from another highly reputable Wall Street bank, J.P. Morgan. The report cited "fears of commoditization and uncertain ROI in new products like lending." Overall, the below dynamic shows that Wall Street analysts are becoming much less optimistic about PYPL with a significantly decreased number of bullish ratings compared to January 2025.

I am not making investment decisions based on the opinions of Wall Street analysts without my own research though. Therefore, let's take a sober view of what is going on.

While PYPL's Q3 earnings were good at the headline numbers level, there was a devil in the detail. Late October earnings release was a double beat, and a 10%+ Q3 EPS surprise was quite wide for a large company like PayPal. Revenue grew by 7.3% YoY and the bottom line also demonstrated growth with the non-GAAP EPS increasing from $1.20 to $1.34. However, I found a few big warning signs in the earnings call. The management's outlook was very cautious for the remainder of 2025 and 2026 full-year because of the macro backdrop and planned investments next year. Moreover, the management pointed out that PayPal's credit business growth is expected to "normalize," which is just a softer word instead of "growth rate decline." Therefore, the strong credit business performance over the last several quarters turned out to be less sustainable than the management likely expected.

Furthermore, the management cited checkout experiences redesign is rolling out slower than expected, which is a headwind that could delay the full realization of benefits from these investments. Finally, the management expects the Q4 transactions margin growth to drop closer to low-single-digits, which will be notably lower than the 7% year-to-date growth.

As we can see, the management's tone is difficult to describe as "confident." With all these warning signs, I think that FY2026 might be quite challenging for PYPL. In addition, competition in the digital payments field is intensifying with both Apple Pay and Google Pay strengthening their foothold. It is just commonsense, the more intense the competition becomes, the more difficult it is to protect the company's pricing power. This has an adverse effect on profitability growth, which we see from PYPL's management's cautious outlook. Amazon is also actively developing and promoting its own digital payments service, which also makes the competition more intense.

Finally, the macroenvironment is indeed challenging. This is a major problem for PYPL because its financial performance directly depends on the total payment volume in the entire economy. According to McKinsey, U.S. consumers feel much less optimistic now compared to the beginning of 2025. I think that deteriorated consumer confidence is reasonable considering increasing U.S. unemployment rate and inflation still above historical averages which means that the Fed cannot afford to cut interest rates significantly. Moreover, the latest reading of the Conference Board Leading Economic Index ("LEI") demonstrated continued decline, implying likely economic slowdown over the next several quarters.

Thus, we can see that there are multiple bearish catalysts brewing, meaning that buying PayPal isn't the best investment decision.

Reasons against PYPL downgrade

The biggest reason not to downgrade PYPL is the fact that it is extremely cheap if we look at the company's valuation ratios. We can see that PYPL is significantly cheaper than the sector median across almost all multiples. The forward Price/FCF ratio of 8.4 while the sector median is 13.8 looks like a real bargain and might attract investors seeking deep value. Other valuation ratios, like the ones with Sales and EBITDA in the formula also look extremely compressed even taking into account all the bearish catalysts.

I think that the discount is too deep because PayPal is still quite strong from the long-term perspective and bearish catalysts mentioned above are mostly temporary than secular. Of course, intensifying competition in digital payments from tech giants is a structural risk. However, the company still delivers revenue and EPS growth even amid intensifying competition, which underscores strong management execution and brand loyalty. Therefore, a share price rebound after a 27% year-to-date dip is also not impossible.

Moreover, this management team has already demonstrated its strong commitment to keeping costs under control. Therefore, PYPL might still continue delivering EPS growth even if revenue growth rates decline. Some efficiency measures might be quite large dollar-wise as the company employs more than 20 thousand employees, and we see that generative AI capabilities are already sophisticated enough to replace at least back-office roles. In case the management announces a large lay-off initiative, investors might start buying PYPL.

Another potential bullish scenario for PYPL is a hypothetical takeover by Apple (AAPL). PayPal's business is still highly profitable, the company boasts a massive user base and a strong brand. PayPal's market cap at the moment is below $60 billion while Apple's TTM free cash flow is almost $80 billion and its total debt is immaterial compare to the $4+ trillion market cap. Therefore, such an acquisition is certainly affordable for Apple. I think that it might be a possible scenario because Apple continues experiencing growth problems as it faces intense competition in the smartphone market. Meanwhile, we also see that Apple is not in the AI infrastructure race as its TTM net capex is at the same level as in 2017. While this scenario's probability is not very high in my opinion as Apple mostly acquires much younger companies, the magnitude of such move on the stock price might be significant given compressed valuation.

The verdict

I think that given the current unfavorable environment, buying more PYPL shares is extremely risky even considering extremely compressed valuation.

data.title: PEO: A High Yield Built On Structure, Not Sentiment
data.description:
Adams Natural Resources Fund (PEO) has a high 9% fwd yield, which amounts to $1.96 in per share payout annualized. They have been paying a dividend for 91 consecutive years. This is a major achievement since they work in the volatile oil and energy sector.

Despite that track record, PEO continues to trade at 9.78% discount to NAV (price ~$21.69 vs. NAV ~$24.04).

PEO manages to get this right because it does not speculate on oil despite being in the energy sector. It only takes the sector’s volatility and converts that into a stable income. It does this through a clear-cut policy structure - its managed distribution policy that pays 8% of NAV year after year:

“The Funds are committed to paying a minimum annual distribution rate of 8% of NAV paid evenly each quarter throughout the year, providing reliability for long-term shareholders.”

The fund also has a default share distribution policy (DRIP - Dividend Reinvestment Plan) whereby shareholders who do not select the cash option automatically get shares issued at a price lower than NAV. This also explains why the fund remains stable and is able to maintain distributions through ups and downs in the sector. Effectively, it makes investors more responsible - towards the fund, and towards their own holdings in it.

PEO’s 95-Year Mandate - A Case Study in Sector Risk Management

Adams Natural Resources Fund was created in 1929 - the year of the Great Depression - and durability has always been its main mandate. It has seen the Depression, a world war, the oil wars era, and today’s climate wars as well. Its closed-end nature has given it the ability to survive it all. How? In a closed-end fund, the capital remains fixed. We buy and sell from one another, not from the fund. That means, fund managers do not need to liquidate shares when shareholders exit. Share prices only move on sentiment, but there is no change in the fund’s holdings. This differs from, say stocks, where both fundamentals and sentiment impact share price.

This stable structure insulates a closed end fund from the inherent volatility of the oil and natural resources sector.

As we have seen, this fund has not only paid dividends for 91 consecutive years, but for the last 74 years, it has also distributed capital gains. This is an unbelievable record - it means the fund has realized profits by selling stocks - every year for the last 74 years. Given that in the last 74 years, we faced the ending years of the 2nd World War, Korea, Vietnam, a series of crises in the Middle East, and so on, it is no surprise that few peer funds can compete with such a record.

How Managed Distribution Actually Works

PEO’s distribution policy is quite mechanical and straightforward: it pays 8% of its average NAV annually, divided into more or less equal quarterly payments. This distribution rate is tied only to NAV, not to dividend income from portfolio companies. They don’t change payout based on whether Exxon is having a bad year, or whether Chevron raised dividends. Instead, the fund meets its distribution through a mix of:

  • Net investment income,

  • Realized long-term gains, and

  • If needed, other allowed sources under CEF rules.

In their 2025 distribution, 30% of their $2.05 YTD payout came from net investment income, and 70% from realized long-term gains. This year, they did not need to touch ROC at all. Now, their interim 19a notices are just estimates. The year-end 1099-DIV determines the real tax character. That’s what tells you how you are going to be taxed. And even if there’s some ROC component to the payout in some years, that’s not always bad. ROC can make perfect sense when the fund is distributing unrealized gains or smoothing payouts in a low-income year, not necessarily eroding NAV.

One more thing: even in a down year when portfolio income fluctuates, the fund can realize gains from long term positions and pay its dividend. An ETF cannot do this because it faces forced redemptions when many investors exit and want cash - perhaps at the worst possible time. In a CEF, the manager can choose when to realize gains and when to hold.

The DRIP system thing is also important here. It needs the shareholders to elect for cash dividends. If they don’t, they get fresh shares, discounted. Long term holders are able to get new shares below NAV in this way. The fund benefits, too - with a stable investor base. Distributions become easier.

All these policies help PEO live through short term swings in the sector. PEO’s structure itself, not the commodity cycle, ensures regular income for investors. And this structure stands on three very stable pillars - a fixed capital setup, controlled realization of gains, and a smooth reinvestment mechanism.

A Portfolio Built to Absorb Commodity Cycles

An analysis of PEO’s portfolio shows us why its payout is so durable across sector cycles. Its holdings are mostly large, durable companies. Their cash flow stays steady even during sharp swings in the sector. The diagram below gives sector breakdown - nearly 80% in energy.

Also from the top ten list, we see that there are no speculative stocks here. The top ten holdings make up 66.28% of assets, and these are companies that stay profitable even during downturns.

These companies do not flow with oil alone. Some of these integrated majors like Exxon and Chevron generate a diversified cash flow, from upstream, downstream and LNG. There are midstream operators here like Williams and Kinder Morgan which earn fee-based contractual revenues. Linde does industrial gas - part of PEO’s materials exposure - and it is tied to global demand from manufacturing and infrastructure rather than oil volatility. Each company acts as an anchor for another one. So when crude prices fall, refining margins improve; when upstream drags, midstream volumes may hold steady; and when the entire energy sector lags, materials can outperform.

PEO’s turnover ratio - which means the percentage of its portfolio it sells every year - is a low ~20%. PEO managers are not running after momentum stocks. They are holding steady positions, only selling when they need funds to distribute. They are not into trading the energy sector, just maintain a steady dividend while absorbing sector shocks themselves.

So, why shouldn’t we simply hold Exxon, Chevron and Williams, and avoid paying PEO their (admittedly small) management fee? We certainly can, but against that small fee, what we get is that we don’t have to handle rebalancing, don’t have to decide when to take profits - and we don’t need to create our own payout policy. PEO does all that for you - for a small 0.90% fee. It does this so well, in fact, that its sister fund Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) allocates a percent of its own NAV to PEO, a subtle internal endorsement of the structure and process at PEO.

Why PEO still trades at a discount

If it is such a beauty, why does PEO still trade at a discount?

Well, the discount doesn’t really imply the portfolio isn’t doing well. It is just a feature common to some CEFs. Since capital is fixed and shares only trade in the secondary market, price goes below NAV when sentiment is lukewarm. But since the payout is tied to NAV, and not share price, the discount does not affect the distribution.

Yes, the next question would be - so why is sentiment lukewarm? Sentiment towards PEO is built on five factors:

  1. Sector sentiment - this itself is lukewarm, with investors moving to tech and AI, the hot sectors now. Energy is a mature and slow-growth sector, and low interest is reflecting on PEO as well, even if PEO itself has historically been lukewarm to energy momentum.

  2. Lack of catalysts- PEO does not do buy backs or tenders, so there are no catalysts to boost the stock.

  3. Conservative design - PEO has a conservative design, mostly low turnover large caps, focused on dependable income, not upsides.

  4. Investor confusion - PEO’s managed distribution policy and DRIP future are often misunderstood. Investors think that the high yield is either not sustainable, or based on ROC, even though there was no ROC at all in 2025, a volatile year.

  5. Sector narrative disconnect - this is tied to the first point. The energy sector is swinging between hope and frustration right now. The bulls are looking at the record cash flows, while the pessimists are thinking that the days of oil dominance are limited, given the focus on ESG and alternative fuel and so on.

Between these five factors, sentiment on PEO stays lukewarm.

That immediately creates a structural advantage for buyers. Say the NAV is 100 and the market price is 90. So when you buy at 90, you buy at a 10% discount to NAV. Now, you get 8 as your dividend, because you get 8% of NAV. But 8 is 8.89% of the market price you bought at. So, your “market yield” is significantly higher than the CEF’s yield because you bought it at a discount. So the discount magnifies your payout.

The discount is also attractive to existing shareholders, who automatically get the option to reinvest their dividends back into the CEF. This stabilizes the shareholder base.

Discount widening, a big issue for income investors, is subtly counteracted by the very discount itself. As the discount stays attractive, more shareholders reinvest via DRIP, so there is a buying pressure which stabilizes the discount.

The discount, therefore, is not an anomaly. It occurs due to sentiment that shouldn’t have been negative, and that makes it attractive for long-term income investors. Shareholders get to buy high quality energy/materials stocks at below intrinsic value, and they also collect a regular dividend that is higher than the 8% official dividend in practice.

Performance Across Oil Cycles

Now that we understand PEO’s structure, how did it actually perform, say, in the last decade, when the energy sector went from oversupply to collapse to post-pandemic rebound? Well, PEO stayed robust, delivering 134.32% total return, almost entirely from distributions, since the 10-year price return is only 21.63%.

The 5-year return of 162.85%, versus 84.00% price return shows how it can stay positive even during the worst times of oil sentiment, and the 3-year return of 31.68% shows how it can recover without using either leverage or speculation.

PEO distributes gains as they are realized, which tends to smooth out the sector’s jagged edges a bit. So you don’t look at the fund purely from a price performance perspective. Share prices may swing, but like I said, the 8-9% yield sobers up the roughness. So long term holders often end up with returns higher than the NAV would imply.

Also noteworthy: PEO doesn’t need any leverage to reach these figures. Many energy CEFs will use 20-30% leverage to amplify returns. PEO remains fully unlevered. So even in sharp downturns, it does not need to resort to forced selling or other corrective measures.

Risks

Despite all its stability and long-term record, PEO has risks. A critical risk comes from its sector concentration. Despite its considerable efforts, PEO cannot always remain insulated from energy volatility. We saw that during the pandemic, when the absolute dollar value of the payout shrank with the NAV.

I discussed the in-built protection against discount widening. However, during deep energy drawdowns, or generally any time investors want to chase higher yielders, that insulation may not hold. It may not affect a NAV-based payout, but it will certainly try investor patience.

We must not also forget that while the 8% NAV payout policy is fine, in a weak NAV situation - and when times are otherwise bad - it may force realization of gains at bad prices. PEO does have decades of experience avoiding that, and can use ROC without destroying NAV, but if that becomes a persistent affair, it might pressure the distribution by eroding NAV.

Finally, PEO is designed as a slow mover, so - no leverage, no buybacks, no hard action. That may look good for most, but those looking for some torque may be disappointed.

Conclusion - buy

If you are looking to ride oil swings, PEO may not be the right vehicle for that. To keep to the analogy, you won’t enjoy the ride, because PEO doesn’t have much torque. It is a rules-based income vehicle that is built to last, not to race. It has run well for nearly one hundred years. It has done so by not pushing itself.

On the other hand, if you are looking for - again the car analogy - a maintenance free vehicle that will keep on going from point A to point B relentlessly, for years and years, PEO is the right choice for you.

It gives a predictable income, so you should treat it as a core income position within energy. If you want Exxon’s cash flow and Williams’ reliability at one place, without much headache - PEO is your man. At its current ~10% discount, ~9% yield, and its extreme reliability, PEO is a compelling buy.

data.title: FactSet: Prone To Lose Market Share
data.description:
Introduction

FactSet Research Systems Inc. (NYSE: FDS) has been one of the biggest losers in 2025, dropping around 39% YTD, while it was initially one of the best compounders on the market. As a previous user of FactSet and an avid user of financial data, I want to dive into why I believe the company is not worth investing in.

FactSet is a provider of financial data, analytics, and various software for investment professionals. It has one of the longest histories in the space and competes directly with the well-renowned Bloomberg Terminal and counts over 237,000 users.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first go through the latest earnings to get a quick glimpse into the company’s operation. FactSet posted a mixed set of earnings with revenue coming in at $596.9MM, a fairly strong growth at 6.2% Y/Y, and a slight beat of $3.46MM. On the bottom line, Q4 Non-GAAP EPS came in at $4.05, a miss of 8 cents. This has pushed the company to have a guidance of Adj. EPS around $16.9 to $17.6 for the next fiscal year. This was deemed relatively conservative by analysts as the company has a high client retention and should see relatively strong growth, though that would only result in a 0% to 2.5% appreciation.

Digging deeper, we notice that FactSet is spending heavily on cloud infrastructure for its FactSet Mercury agent, which is basically the company’s AI solution. This means that margins have been compressed, and the trend should continue in the near future. The argument is that they need to spend that much to defend their position against modern startups, as well as Bloomberg and Refinitiv, the latter heavily spending into AI. The company is also pivoting from traditionally selling to portfolio managers to now focusing on Enterprise solutions.

FactSet has also been following the trend in the AI narrative with striking major strategic partnerships, such as an AWS collaboration with the company’s market intelligence being tapped for Amazon Quick Research, which will make their data way more accessible. FactSet will also create a unified platform with Arcesium that will connect both the Front Office and the Back Office for asset managers. These are all interesting, but I do not believe that it will significantly move the needle for the company.

Valuation

Factset is on the lower price side compared to its closest peers (W/Bloomberg), with its current P/E of 16.98, that has been compressed due to the stock’s decline. It comes well below peers such as Morningstar and S&P. There is a lot of potential reasons for this, such as the incoming gap year in terms of earnings, as well as the compression in margins with the market in a wait-and-see mode to see if the FDS’s AI venture will pay off. Right now, the company has a fairly good FCF yield at around 5.9%, the highest level over the past 10 years.

Growth is the main story right now with the current shift in dynamics, though I have to say that the company has done a strong job over the past 3 years in both growing its top line, at 7.98% CAGR, while net income reached 14.58% CAGR. It is important to note that a lot has to do with the CUSIP acquisition for nearly $2B that added reliable tracking for North American securities that has the dominant position in the space. However, now the company needs to grow organically, and many of its clients, such as major investment banks, are making critical cuts in terms of employees, meaning that they require fewer logins.

Right now, FactSet is trading short-term profitability for long-term survival as they are accepting near-term margin compression compared to the broad industry. It is generally trailing peers in margins, with an EBITDA margin of 39.02%, while MSCI is currently 57.95% and Moody’s at 47.71%. Though I’ll say that FactSet is doing a strong job at translating it into FCF with a current margin of 26.6% thanks to the nature of the product. The company is also highly capital efficient with its ROE of 28.6% and ROCE nearing 20%, at 19.8%.

My Reasons For Skepticism

I’ll first start with my personal experience as I’ve had the chance to use Refinitiv, Bloomberg, and FactSet. Although in the end they all have similar offerings, as the data itself is always the same, FactSet was always deemed as the worst product offering. It had the least favorite interface and could face some issues in integration for Excel modelling. Furthermore, FactSet will never have the same kind of association and brand recognition that Bloomberg has, which allows it to dominate the market and charge the highest prices, mainly for its chat functionalities.

FactSet is also generally the cheapest of the premium data providers, which leaves it even more vulnerable to the rise of startups in the space. Companies like Seeking Alpha can directly rival FactSet for a fraction of the cost for investors and newcomers to the industry. The data feed is important, but the company’s legacy business, in my mind, will not be able to generate the same levels of revenue as before. This leaves the positioning of FactSet in a predicament, as either consumers are going to go towards a cheaper data provider, as the added value of FactSet simply does not make sense for nearly $1,000 a month, or professionals will only stick to using a Bloomberg terminal. While I was at Euronext, the only reason we had FactSet licences was because the company allowed password sharing and same time uses, which highlights another failure on the company’s side.

Another point I want to add regarding FactSet’s vulnerabilities, the cheapest premium offering would be its sales department, or more so, how it is inferior to the two other main providers (I have no experience with S&P Capital IQ). When I wanted to launch a particular venture that required direct access to a premium data provider, I reached out to all three with Bloomberg calling me in a minute and offering a one-month free trial while Refinitiv had the best customer relation with weekly meetings in functionalities and offering a 2-month free trial to test out the product and give me promotions for the first year in order to scale at a cheaper cost. Now these are examples of how to gain customers and cater to them with software that is highly expensive. On the FactSet side, well, crickets. They replied over 6 months later, and when I directly answered back, I never heard from them again. This simple example is probably not a generality, but I want to highlight what separates the top of the industry from FactSet, to which it is generally compared.

Conclusion

I just believe that FactSet is not worth investing into, especially as you compare it to a company like the London Stock Exchange that owns Refinitiv and also all the derived stock market operations. Even if FDS could maintain its ground against competitors like Bloomberg, it would be one of the first to lose market share against up-and-coming companies in the space that are offering great prices for nearly the same functionalities. I rate FactSet as a Sell.

data.title: PMMF: Growth Rebound Calms Growth Mandate Response Forecast
data.description:
The iShares Prime Money Market ETF (PMMF) is a way to take duration risk out of the picture. The issue is with low duration bets we are fine with a little bit of credit risk, and would prefer more yield, and also lower expense ratios, with a similarly low duration and curated portfolio in (FLOT). We are in general quite fine with shorter durations. While we didn't necessarily expect the latest cut, we think the signs are there that further cuts might be unlikely due to the extent of dissent from people currently not in the FOMC but who will end up in it. Moreover, the yield curve reflects the comments of Powell of a wait and see stance for now, with the expectations that there are still drivers for higher long-term rates. It's hard to say whether the curve will shift up at this point and affect longer duration bets, but we do think that taking short duration, and indeed some modest credit risk might make sense as unemployment stays moderate and growth rebounds. Therefore, we prefer FLOT over something like the PMMF, even if we like the shorter durations.

PMMF Breakdown

The expense ratio is 0.2%, and the ETF engages in a strategy that achieves the objective of being a money market instrument portfolio, meaning that the underlying is highly liquid and offers effectively spot rates cumulative over a holding period. There is technically no or basically no credit risk to the extent that one would consider debt with institutions like the US Government a no risk investment.

There is no duration risk at least, meaning that shifts in the yield curve don't affect the value of the underlying. The ETF pays out payouts on a monthly basis.

While there are plenty of instruments that offer low credit risk options for fixed income investors, more interesting is the decision to eliminate any duration risk. There are several reasons why an investor may choose to do that at this point in time, reasons that we'd rather agree with from an asset allocation point of view.

  • There's been another rate cut which would be good for short to intermediate duration bets that were affected by the consequent downshift in the US yield curve.
  • However, the probability of further imminent cuts is limited by several factors, including Powell's comments affirming a wait-and-see stance at this point, also reflecting considerable dissent from Federal Reserve system bankers outside of the current rotation of voting bankers.
  • Issues at hand include the fact that inflation is still running quite hot and was reinflating, with the latest data putting it at the upper band level for where policy action is usually considered warranted (3%).
  • On the other hand, concerning the growth mandate, while unemployment has not ticked down, growth is seeming to rebound a little bit which may indicate that the need to tend to the growth mandate at this moment is lesser than before.

In other words, staying with current rates, perhaps even raising them, which is what the yield curve seems to imply will likely happen in the medium-term, deals with the hotter inflation and the momentarily addressed growth mandate. Markets seem to have adopted this view with actually a steepening of the yield curve on the longer part, probably reflecting concerns that inflation may persist and anchor at higher levels requiring higher long-term rates.

There are other issues at play. President Trump is looking to replace Powell and that is expected to occur very soon. Fed independence may be a little bit of a question at this point, which has implications for the institution's credibility and therefore long-term rates. With the same effect but a contrary rationale, a new chair of the Fed may struggle more to lead more rate cuts due to the dissent.

Bottom Line

We share the market's concern with the rate path down from here. Therefore, we are also not keen on duration. However, with rebounding growth we are less worried about credit risks in line with markets which are compressing single-A credit risk premiums. FLOT, by taking on a little bit of credit risk at around AA and A ratings, achieves considerably stronger yields at 4.48% on the 30-day SEC yield basis compared to 3.96%, consistent with the average credit risk profile. Moreover, it has a 0.15% expense ratio, lower than PMMF. At short durations this credit risk is also less of a concern as issues that would affect imminent liquidity due dates for lenders are less likely. We'd go with FLOT from an allocation perspective.

data.title: S&P 500's Outlook For 2026: Don't Expect This Rally To Fade
data.description:
Out-Of-Consensus Thinking For The S&P 500 (SPY)

If we take a look at what this decade has given us so far, we'll notice that most outlooks failed, and the market was making what the consensus wasn't pricing in. For example, I recall how most investors were scared of the COVID effect on the market back in 2020, even when the S&P 500 fell by over 35% off its high at the time. Or in 2021, when inflation came in, it also surprised many. It seemed logical that the Fed would have begun an aggressive rate-hiking cycle, but when it did, the market hadn't been ready for it. Then, in 2023, the majority of analysts I read were expecting at least a mild recession (and I was thinking the same way, too), but it didn't come. In 2025, when Trump took the office, a lot of people were bullish on equities (and crypto) because of the tax plans of the new administration (to name just one of the reasons). But then the Liberation Day came, and the S&P 500 lost about 20%. Now, on a YTD basis, it's up by 17.59% at the time of this writing, and I hear how people are getting fearful of the AI bubble hiding in plain sight. And I think that these fears have become a consensus narrative these days, and that this consensus is likely to disappoint if you're betting on it. From what I see, SPY is very likely to end 2026 with a positive gain, inside the range that I'll outline at the end of this article.

I Think The AI Bubble Won't Pop In 2026

The main argument from my end is that the macro isn't telling us that the market is topping next year. First, the inflation, which the Fed fought and won a couple of years ago, doesn't seem to be coming back again as some bears are expecting. The 5-year breakeven inflation has been around the low-2% area recently, based on FRED data, and the 5y/5y forward inflation expectations have also been belo 2.5% area since October 2023:

And the bond markets are not cautious. Take a look at the investment-grade corporate spreads, which have remained below 1% since May 2025. Their previous local rise took place in April of this year, and we can recall what drove that (the Liberation Day). But even back then, the credit spread there failed to go beyond 1.25. So, even then, we were in a much better position than in, say, 2019, or most years of the past decade.

I should make it clear here that tight credit spreads don't necessarily mean that the risks are nonexistent. But they mean the market isn't pricing near-term default stress, and it wasn't pricing in anything of this kind even in April 2025. A lot of investors are scared that Oracle's (ORCL) needs to raise a lot of new debt to finance its projects, and the obligation to service the debt will ruin its business model and ultimately lead to insolvency. That's why its CDS are rising so rapidly in recent weeks. But I believe it's an idiosyncratic event, and it has nothing to do with the equity market in general. If the credit side of the macro setup was worried about 2026, we'd have seen its voting right now. It just doesn't happen.

Also, the overheated valuation has become a real concern. I was also flagging this risk in some of my past articles on SPY. What SPY bears point to lately is the rise in the CAPE ratio. But if they're right, history tells us that SPY has at least one more year to run higher before the bubble, if there's any, pops.

And analysts at Deutsche Bank agree with my point:

And even if you think we’re in a bubble, it’s worth noting that during the dot com bubble, the rally went on for another year after the CAPE ratio had got to current levels.

Source: Deutsche Bank's report (proprietary source, December 2025)

What can drive earnings higher for the biggest companies within the S&P 500 is the efficiency gains from AI implementation. UBS macro analysts, whom I read regularly (proprietary source), recently wrote that the upside tail from AI capex is likely to be bigger than people want to admit, and I tend to agree with them.

While industrialization mainly artificially multiplied the physical strength of workers, AI is about increasing the productivity of the human brain. This is also reflected in the acronym of the "BRAIN Revolution," which stands for Biotechnology, Robotics, AI, and Nanotechnology. In this respect, AI goes far beyond the First Industrial Revolution 150 years ago. Indeed, it could make the current bull market the largest of a generation, by sustainably increasing not only the productivity of the "Magnificent 7," but especially that of the remaining "Impressive 493."

Source: UBS, "Christmas Rally" report, proprietary source

We're in an active CAPEX phase, which usually takes just a few years before we see any material outcomes. A lot of skeptics ask about ROIC already, which I suppose may be too early to predict because the CAPEX cycle itself is far from over.

And only a few people are asking, "What if that CAPEX leads to a higher-than-usual US real GDP growth rate?" Again, assuming the inflation risks are under control, a boost in GDP growth rates would send us back to the mid-90s of the past century, not the early 2000s.

Between Q2 1996 and Q3 2000, every quarter saw US growth at an annualised rate of over 4% with an average rate around 4.5%. In this cycle, US growth has been strong since Q1 2023 but has only averaged 2.6% annualised. Could AI Capex and productivity gains take us back to those rates from the late-1990s, whether prolonged or temporarily due to a bubble?

Source: Deutsche Bank's report (proprietary source, December 2025)

So, overall, despite some clear signs that SPY and most other indices are overheated, the macro conditions look bullish if we think about 2026.

My S&P 500 Price Target For 2026

It's probably easier to come up with a price target leaning on the price-to-earnings ratio with an EPS assumption. Here's what the consensus estimate is telling us right now:

The consensus earnings growth estimate for S&P 500 companies in 2025 compared to 2024 is 10%. The growth estimate for 2026 compared to 2025 is 13.2% for S&P 500 companies.

Source: ETF Trend's data, emphasis added by Oakoff

So, next year is going to outpace 2025 in terms of the EPS expansion, and that's why I believe the currently elevated P/E ratio for the S&P 500 isn't going anywhere.

I won't take 31.23x exactly, but I'll stick to the range of 25-30x, with the midpoint being my base case projection.

The FactSet's data says that for FY2026, the bottom-up EPS estimate for the S&P 500 is $309.22, so I'm going to assume that the actual EPS will come in with a 3% premium, taking into account the past quarters' beating success.

Here's what I get for each of my 3 scenarios:

Base case = $309.22*1.03*27.5x = $8,758.66

Bull case = $309.22*1.06*30x = $9,833.20

Bear case = $309.22*0.97*25x = $7,498.56

Source: Oakoff's calculations

In the base, bull, and bear cases, the S&P 500 has upside potential of 26.94%, 42.51%, and 8.67%. Given that even in my bear case scenario, the S&P 500 is projected to rise, I'm upgrading my previous "Hold" for the index to "Buy".

Risks

As you could have seen above, I base my findings on the presumption that macro looks favorable for the stock market, which may not be true. Macroeconomics, in general, is a very complicated story with a lot of puzzles that are hard to connect. So, I may be missing something in my today's update. Also, in case the AI CAPEX we're seeing now becomes a short-lived spending burst rather than a durable productivity engine, the S&P 500 and most other indices will likely reprice to at least their average historical multiples. Speaking about SPY specifically, in case the actual FY2026 EPS comes with a discount of 5% to the current consensus level, and the market decides to value it with a P/E of only 20x, the year-end price target would reach only $5,875:

$309.22 * 0.95 * 20x = $5,875.18 (a downside of 14.58%)

Source: Oakoff's calculations

Your Takeaway

I believe that the whole narrative around the "AI bubble" in the market right now is not matching the reality, and that even if there's a bubble, 2026 won't be the year it pops. The key macro fundamentals point to a mild risk profile, hinting at a likely bullish run next year. Based on my model, using a reasonable band of outcomes for both EPS and the forward multiple, I arrive at an end-2026 S&P 500 range of roughly 7,500-9,800, with ~$8,760 as my base case. That's why I'm comfortable upgrading my view to "Buy" despite the obvious overheating concerns.

Good luck with your investments!
